message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  SARS-CoV-2-specific memory T cells that cross-react with common cold coronaviruses (CCCs) are present in both healthy donors and COVID-19 patients. However, whether these cross-reactive T cells play a role in COVID-19 pathogenesis versus protection remain to be fully elucidated. In this study, we characterized cross-reactive SARS-CoV-2-specific CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cells, targeting genome-wide conserved epitopes in a cohort of 147 non-vaccinated COVID-19 patients, divided into six groups based on the degrees of disease severity. We compared the frequency, phenotype, and function of these SARS-CoV-2-specific CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cells between severely ill and asymptomatic COVID-19 patients and correlated this with α-CCCs and β-CCCs co-infection status. Compared with asymptomatic COVID-19 patients, the severely ill COVID-19 patients and patients with fatal outcomes: (
                  <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                  ) Presented a broad leukocytosis and a broad CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cell lymphopenia; (
                  <jats:italic>ii</jats:italic>
                  ) Developed low frequencies of functional IFN-
                  <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                  -producing CD134
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD138
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and CD134
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD138
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cells directed toward conserved epitopes from structural, non-structural and regulatory SARS-CoV-2 proteins; (
                  <jats:italic>iii</jats:italic>
                  ) Displayed high frequencies of SARS-CoV-2-specific functionally exhausted PD-1
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  TIM3
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  TIGIT
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CTLA4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and PD-1
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  TIM3
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  TIGIT
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CTLA4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cells; and (
                  <jats:italic>iv</jats:italic>
                  ) Displayed similar frequencies of co-infections with β-CCCs strains but significantly fewer co-infections with α-CCCs strains. Interestingly, the cross-reactive SARS-CoV-2 epitopes that recalled the strongest CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cell responses in unexposed healthy donors (HD) were the most strongly associated with better disease outcome seen in asymptomatic COVID-19 patients. Our results demonstrate that, the critically ill COVID-19 patients displayed fewer co-infection with α-CCCs strain, presented broad T cell lymphopenia and higher frequencies of cross-reactive exhausted SARS-CoV-2-specific CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cells. In contrast, the asymptomatic COVID-19 patients, appeared to present more co-infections with α-CCCs strains, associated with higher frequencies of functional cross-reactive SARS-CoV-2-specific CD4
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  and CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cells. These findings support the development of broadly protective, T-cell-based, multi-antigen universal pan-Coronavirus vaccines.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec id="s1">
                  <jats:title>KEY POINTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        A broad lymphopenia and lower frequencies of SARS-CoV-2-specific CD4
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        and CD8
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        T-cells were associated with severe disease onset in COVID-19 patients.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        High frequencies of phenotypically and functionally exhausted SARS-CoV-2-specific CD4
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        and CD8
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        T cells, co-expressing multiple exhaustion markers, and targeting multiple structural, non-structural, and regulatory SARS-CoV-2 protein antigens, were detected in severely ill COVID-19 patients.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        Compared to severely ill COVID-19 patients and to patients with fatal outcomes, the (non-vaccinated) asymptomatic COVID-19 patients presented more functional cross-reactive CD4
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        and CD8
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        T cells targeting conserved epitopes from structural, non-structural, and regulatory SARS-CoV-2 protein antigens.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        The cross-reactive SARS-CoV-2 epitopes that recalled the strongest CD4
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        and CD8
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        T cell responses in unexposed healthy donors (HD) were the most strongly associated with better disease outcomes seen in asymptomatic COVID-19 patients.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Compared to severely ill COVID-19 patients and to patients with fatal outcomes, the (non-vaccinated) asymptomatic COVID-19 patients presented higher rates of co-infection with the α-CCCs strains.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        Compared to patients with mild or asymptomatic COVID-19, severely ill symptomatic patients and patients with fatal outcomes had more exhausted SARS-CoV-2-speccific CD4
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        and CD8
                        <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                        T cells that preferentially target cross-reactive epitopes that share high identity and similarity with the β-CCCs strains.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title:
High Frequencies of PD-1
                  <sup>+</sup>
                  TIM3
                  <sup>+</sup>
                  TIGIT
                  <sup>+</sup>
                  CTLA4
                  <sup>+</sup>
                  Functionally Exhausted SARS-CoV-2-Specific CD4
                  <sup>+</sup>
                  and CD8
                  <sup>+</sup>
                  T Cells Associated with Severe Disease in Critically ill COVID-19 Patients
